coredata.dc:title: Lemon juice-derived nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive and selective determination of ferrous ions and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yue
Li, Zhenjiang
Sheng, Liying
Meng, Alan
coredata.dc:description: Ferrous ion (Fe2+) plays an important role in a large number of chemical and biological processes in living organisms. So, the accurate and rapid determination of Fe2+ in environmental and biological systems is an important issue. In this paper, N-CDs with uniform particle size, outstanding water solubility, favorable stability, high quantum yield (QY, 58.66 %) and excellent photoluminescence performance were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass lemon and ethylene diamine. The N-CDs showed a bright blue fluorescence under UV light. This new type of N-CDs can be used to effectively detect Fe2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. It displayed a fine linear relationship in the range of 2–625 μΜ with the LOD of 0.063 μM under optimum conditions. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism can be ascribed to the static quenching (SQ) and internal filtration effect (IFE) for Fe2+. More importantly, the optical imaging performance and cytotoxicity of N-CDs were also demonstrated. The results showed that N-CDs owned low cytotoxicity and glorious biocompatibility, and could be used as a biological imaging agent for labeling Hela cells. Therefore, this study possesses expansive application prospects in environmental monitoring and bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Fe2+ detection
Low cytotoxicity
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Fe2+ in tap water
Detection of Fe2+ ion in tap water
MTT assay and application of cell imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, 52102106 , Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09 , the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, ZR2020QE063 , the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004 , and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034 . We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: histogram for size distribution) and (b) HRTEM of N-CDs, (c) Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of N-CDs and mapping images of C(d), N(e), O(f).

(a) The FL spectra of N-CDs before and after the adding of Fe2+, (insets: the images of N-CDs before and after the addition of Fe2+ under 365 nm UV light), (b) The FL spectra of N-CDs after the addition of Fe2+ at different response time, (c) FL spectra of N-CDs solution after addition of different concentrations of Fe2+, (d) Fitting curve of the ﬂuorescence intensity versus Fe2+ concentration from 0 to 625 μM (inset: the linear curve for Fe2+ detection).

(a) Selectivity of prepared N-CDs for various heavy metal ions, (b) FL quenching response of N-CDs to Fe2+ ions in the addition of other cations, (c) The normalized maximum excitation, emission, ﬂuorescence spectra of N-CDs and UV spectrum of Fe2+, N-CDs in the presence of Fe2+, (d) The FL decay time of N-CDs before and after the adding of Fe2+.
Confocal microscopy FL image of Hela cells incubated (37 °C, 5 h) with N-CDs under diﬀerent excitation wavelengths (405 and 488 nm) and corresponding bright-field images as well as merged images.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis N-CDs and their application in Fe2+ detection and cell imaging.

head.ce:title: Lemon juice-derived nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive and selective determination of ferrous ions and cell imaging

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, Shandong Province 266042, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferrous ion (Fe2+) plays an important role in a large number of chemical and biological processes in living organisms. So, the accurate and rapid determination of Fe2+ in environmental and biological systems is an important issue. In this paper, N-CDs with uniform particle size, outstanding water solubility, favorable stability, high quantum yield (QY, 58.66 %) and excellent photoluminescence performance were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using biomass lemon and ethylene diamine. The N-CDs showed a bright blue fluorescence under UV light. This new type of N-CDs can be used to effectively detect Fe2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. It displayed a fine linear relationship in the range of 2–625 μΜ with the LOD of 0.063 μM under optimum conditions. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism can be ascribed to the static quenching (SQ) and internal filtration effect (IFE) for Fe2+. More importantly, the optical imaging performance and cytotoxicity of N-CDs were also demonstrated. The results showed that N-CDs owned low cytotoxicity and glorious biocompatibility, and could be used as a biological imaging agent for labeling Hela cells. Therefore, this study possesses expansive application prospects in environmental monitoring and bioanalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an essential trace element for human body and the most abundant transition metal element. It is almost distributed in all organs and tissues of human body, with a total content of about 45 mg [1]. Iron is essential to living systems and plays a vital role in many physiological processes. It not only binds to hemoglobin in the body to transport oxygen from the lungs to the key cell, but also helps transfer electrons and respiration as part of cytochrome and respiratory enzymes. Iron helps maintain normal hematopoiesis by promoting the formation and maturation of red blood cells. It also promotes brain development and improves brain function. Although iron is an extremely important component of life forms, excessive accumulation of iron in the central nervous system can cause a number of serious problems, such as anemia, growth inhibition, cardiovascular disease, liver damage, kidney metabolic diseases, iron pneumoconiosis, Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease in the elderly [2–4]. Trace amounts of Fe2+ in the cytoplasm is a probable catalyst of the Fenton reaction, producing highly oxidizing hydroxyl radicals (∙OH), which are the most dangerous reactive oxygen species in biological processes, resulting in irreparable DNA damage. In addition, Fe2+ deficiency is common in infancy, adolescence and pregnancy, and can lead to a range of conditions, including anemia, lymphocytopenia, reduced ability to work, and reduced resistance to infection and cold. In order to prevent or improve Fe2+ deficiency, people often take some Fe2+ supplements or iron-rich food, because iron bivalent is more easily absorbed than iron trivalent, iron agent is generally iron bivalent, such as ferrous sulfate tablets, ferrous fumarate tablets, vitamin iron sustained-release tablets, etc. In a word, the research of simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost Fe2+ detection methods is of great significance in the fields of pathology, physiology and biology.

At present, a plethora of techniques for quantitative monitor iron ions have been developed, including colorimetric [5,6], atomic absorption spectrometry [7], electrochemistry [8,9], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [10], fluorescence [11–15] and so on. In the above methods, fluorescent approache has drawn increasing attention due to its advantages of simple making, high sensitivity, selectivity, fast detection and particularly the variety of newly emerging fluorescent probes. However, the fluorescence methods used for the detection of Fe2+ are still very few. Therefore, it is very important to develop a new fluorescence assay for the detection of Fe2+.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs), as a famous new zero-dimensional carbon material, have been attracted extensive attention [16]. Compared with other fluorescent substances, CDs possess high fluorescence intensity, excellent water solubility, chemical inertness, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [17]. Based on these characteristics, CDs can be exploited as promising candidates in great potential application including solar cell, fluorescent sensor, bioimaging, and more [18]. To date, a variety of techniques have been developed to prepare CDs [19]. Among them, the hydrothermal techniques for preparation CDs using low-cost and environment-friendly biomass including tomatoes, ginkgo leaves, milk, garlic and pomelo peel as carbon source has drawn immense attention attribute to their green chemistry nature [20]. However, one of the major problems that CDs fabricated using these biomasses is the low quantum yield (QY), which limits their applications. To overcome these defects, it is urgently needed to regulate core and surface microstructures of CDs. Doping is an effective strategy to adjust the intrinsic qualities of CDs, such as electronic, surface and local chemical reactivity, etc. Bringing in heteroatoms, especially introducing nitrogen, can change the electronic structure of CDs and improve QY. To acquire nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs), two main strategies (top-down and bottom-up approaches) are employed [21]. For the top-down approach, N-CDs were synthesized using biomasses containing nitrogen element in themselves. However, the QY of N-CDs fabricated via this strategy is still no significantly improve [22]. On account of this, the bottom-up approach is adopted to fabricate N-CDs with a high QY through the hydrothermal techniques using biomasses carbon sources and nitrogen-rich molecular. As far as we know, there is relatively scarcely reports on utilizing biomass as carbon sources to the preparation of N-CDs with high QY for assaying Fe2+ and cell imaging.

In this paper, for the first time, biomass lemon juice was attempted as the carbon source and ethanediamine as a nitrogen doping precursor for fabrication highly efficient via a hydrothermal technique. The optical performance of the synthesized N-CDs have been exploited through UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Furthermore, the synthesized N-CDs have been investigated as a fluorescence probe for rapid, selective and sensitive determination Fe2+ by monitoring the quenching the fluorescence intensity and the quenching mechanism has been explored. More importantly, it could also be used in assay Fe2+ in tap water sample and display high sensitivity. Also, the N-CDs can be applied as bioimaging agent for the multicolor fluorescence imaging of Hela cells. Scheme 1 shows the illustration for the preparation N-CDs and their application.
Reagents and apparatus used for the experiments in Supplementary material.
The detailed synthesis methods are shown in Supplementary material.

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) were measured using the Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ
C D s
=
Φ
R
(
I
C D s
/ I
R
)
(
A
R
/
A
C D s
)
(
η
C D s
/
η
R
)
2
Where Ф is the QY, I stands for ﬂuorescence emission intensity, A represents the absorbance and η is referred as the refractive index of the solvent, the subscript CDs and R indicate nitrogen doped CDs and reference, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (the quantum yield of 54 %) was used as a standard [23–25].

Fe2+ were detected at room temperature using N-CDs. All N-CDs solutions were diluted 140 times with deionized water before use. All fluorescence detection were conducted at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and emission wavelength of 440 nm. The excitation and emission slit width are both 5 nm. Firstly, a series of concentrations Fe2+ aqueous solutions (0–10−2 M) were prepared. 0.15 mL Fe2+ aqueous solutions with different concentrations were mixed with 0.3 mL N-CDs solution, respectively. After 5 min at room temperature, the photoluminescence spectrum was detected with fluorescence spectrophotometer to study the sensitivity of N-CDs towards Fe2+ in the solution. For selectivity, 0.15 mL metal ion solutions (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Sn4+, Zn2+) were added to 0.3 mL N-CDs solution, respectively. After mixing evenly, the photoluminescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. In the anti-interference experiment, 0.45 mL N-CDs solution containing Fe2+ (10−2 M) were mixed with 0.15 mL other metal ion solutions (Cu+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Sn4+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ni2+) and the photoluminescence spectrum was recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer after incubation at room temperature for 5 min. Three repeated experiments were carried out for the determination of its sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe2+ in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs probe was utilized to determine Fe2+ in tap water samples. First, the tap water sample was pretreated. Then, standard Fe2+ solutions of different concentrations (5, 10, 20, 50, 100 μM) were injected into the tap water samples, respectively. Finally, Fe2+ in samples was tested by the standard addition recovery approach.

Besides, the sample was determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was determined by repeating 3 times under the same experimental conditions.

To further investigate the cytotoxicity of N-CDs, MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2–2thiazolyl)−2,5-diphenyl-2 H-tetrazoliumbromide) assay was performed using Hela cells. First, Hela cells (8 × 103/well) was inoculated into 96 well plates and cultured at a constant temperature incubator (5 % CO2, 37 °C) for 24 h. Then, different concentrations of N-CDs were added to each well and incubated for another 5 h. After that, the medium containing the sample was removed and each well was cleaned using PBS for three times. Subsequently, MTT reagent (0.5 mg/mL, 100 μL) were added into each well and cultured at a constant temperature incubator (5 % CO2, 37 °C). After incubation for another 4 h, the supernatant was carefully removed, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 100 μL) was added to each well containing MTT. Finally, the mixture was gently shaken for 10 min and the absorbance of the solution at 570 nm was recorded. The cell viability value was calculated using to the Eq. (2):
(2)
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y ( % ) =
(
O
D
t r e a t e d
O
D
c o n t r o l
)
× 100 %
Where OD
treated is the absorbance in the presence of sample, OD
control is the absorbance in the absence of N-CDs sample.

Hela cells incubated in MEM medium containing fetal bovine serum (5 % CO2, 37 °C) were seeded in a special confocal dish at a density of 20 × 104 for each dish and incubated at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator for 24 h, then incubated with N-CDs in a cell culture dish for 5 h. After the incubation, the Hela cells were washed with PBS for three times and the culture medium was added. The FL images of Hela cells were filmed under the laser scanning confocal microscope with excitation wavelengths of 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively.

N-CDs with the excellent fluorescence property were fabricated by optimizing the preparation process parameters including reaction temperature, reaction time and amounts of ethylenediamine. Respective text and Figures on majorizations were showed in Fig. S1 (in Supplementary Material). In addition, N-CDs was diluted 140 times as the optimal concentration, the optimal results and analysis was shown in Fig. S2. The optical performance tests in the subsequent experiments were carried out at this concentration.

The size and morphology of the synthesized N-CDs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1a shows TEM image of N-CDs, the N-CDs are monodispersed nanoparticles with spherical morphology, and the particle size distribution is uniform, without obvious aggregation. The histogram inserted in Fig. 1a shows that the size of these N-CDs is diminutive, the particle size distribution is between 2.3 and 3.5 nm, and the average particle size is about 2.9 nm (random statistics of more than 50 nanoparticles). It can be observed from HRTEM image that N-CDs takes on clear lattice stripes with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in Fig. 1b, which conforms to (100) lattice plane of typical graphene structure.

EDS analysis of element composition (Fig. 1c) showed the existence of C, N and O elements in the synthesized products. Moreover, the element mapping (Fig. 1d–f) explicitly shows that elements C, N and O are evenly distributed on N-CDs. Among them, C and N come from N-CDs, and O may come from functional groups on the surface of N-CDs. The aforementioned results further demonstrated the successful synthesis of N-CDs.

The crystallinity of the as-synthesized N-CDs was analyzed by XRD. The XRD spectrum showed a wide and strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 26° in Fig. S3a, conforming to (002) plane of graphite material [26]. There is a faint peak at 2θ = 42°, which corresponds to (100) plane of graphite material. This is basically consistent with research results of TEM, indicating that N-CDs possess graphite-like structure. The surface functional groups of N-CDs were studied via FT-IR. As shown in Fig. S3b, the wide absorption peak at 3302 cm−1 proved the presence of the stretching vibration peak of O-H and N-H, respectively. The peaks centered at 2974 cm−1 and 2879 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of C-H. The strong absorption band at 1637 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO bond, and the peak at 1556 cm−1 can be attributed to the N-H stretching vibration. The peak at 1448 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CC bond, and the bands peaked at 1385 cm−1, 1329 cm−1 and 1075 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of COO-, C-N and C-O bonds, respectively. These results show that the N-CDs surface has rich functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and carbonyl functional groups, which make N-CDs possess fine water solubility and is conducive to improve their fluorescence properties.

Besides, the surface chemical composition of the N-CDs was determined using XPS spectroscopy. It can be seen from Fig. S3c, the XPS full spectrum of N-CDs has three distinct peaks at 284.83, 399.73 and 531.08 eV, which belong to C1s (69.08 %), N1s (15.39 %) and O1s (15.53 %) [27], respectively. High resolution XPS spectra further confirmed the corresponding binding energy. High resolution C1s XPS data (Fig. S3d) shows three peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.13 eV and 287.84 eV, which are attributed to C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and -COOH, respectively [28,29]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S3e) can be decomposed into two main peaks at 399.58 eV [30] and 400.81 eV [31], according with C-N-H and C-N-C bonds, respectively, indicating that N is doped into CDs. Fig. S3f demonstrates the high-resolution O1s spectrum of N-CDs, which manifested two bonding states: CO (531.05 eV) [32] and C-O (532.31 eV) [33]. XPS analysis showed that there were oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups in the synthesized N-CDs, which was identified with the results of FT-IR spectrum. Due to the diversity of its surface functional groups, N-CDs has glorious dispersion and hydrophilicity in water, as well as stable fluorescence properties.

In order to explore the optical characteristics of N-CDs, the ﬂuorescent emission, UV–vis absorption, pH performance, and the stability under all kinds of conditions were explored. As shown in Fig. S4a, emission spectra are presented at different excitation wavelengths (300–440 nm). With the variation of excitation wavelength (300–360 nm), the fluorescence emission intensity increases. The maximum emission intensity is obtained at 440 nm when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. A further increase in the excitation wavelength (380–440 nm) leads to a decrease in the emission intensity. Furthermore, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength moves to a longer wavelength (red shift). It is speculated that the excitation dependent emission characteristics of N-CDs may be due to the different emission sites caused by surface defects or uneven heterogeneity of size. In addition, taking quinine sulfate as the reference, the QY of N-CDs was calculated to be 58.66 %, which was higher than that of carbon quantum dots synthesized with other biomass as precursors [27,30,34–36].

Fig. S4b demonstrates the UV-Vis spectra of N-CDs before and after the adding of Fe2+. The N-CDs has a typical absorption peak at 280 nm, which can extend to the visible region, corresponding to the n-π * electronic transition of CO bond in oxygen-containing group [34,37], which may come from hydroxyl (-COOH) on the surface of N-CDs. Under UV irradiation, N-CDs aqueous solution shows blue fluorescence, explaining that the as-prepared N-CDs has bright luminescence characteristics, while under sunlight, it is brownish-yellow to the naked eye (Fig. S4b inset). Besides, there is no significant distinction in the UV spectra of N-CDs without and with Fe2+, indicating that they have similar UV characteristics.

To study the feasibility of N-CDs as a fluorescent marker in the actual environment, we explored the effects of pH value on the FL intensity of N-CDs aqueous. The FL changes under different pH (2–12) conditions were observed in Fig. S4c. When the pH value of N-CDs was lower than 5 and higher than 8, the FL intensity showed a downward trend. When the pH value was 8, the FL intensity reached the maximum value, and the fluorescence intensity fluctuated less when the pH value was 5–8. For a more intuitive understanding of the change of FL intensity with pH, the corresponding scatter plot was drawn in the inset, it can be seen that pH between 5 and 8 has an excellent stability. Considering that the pH value of physiological environment is usually 7–8, it is contribute to the biological imaging application of N-CDs. Besides, FL emission peak shifted red in acidic solution. This pH sensitive behavior may be due to that N-CDs may exist in an isolated form in aqueous solution at low pH; with the rise of pH value, the interaction of non-covalent bonds such as hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups is enhanced, which may lead to the aggregation of N-CDs [38].

In order to assess FL stability of N-CDs, the FL intensity in different concentrations (0–2.0 M) NaCl solution was carried out. As shown in Fig. S4d, with the increase of the ionic intensity of NaCl solution, the position and shape of the peak remain unchanged, especially, when the NaCl concentration is as high as 2.0 M, FL intensity of N-CDs still does not variation significantly, indicating that N-CDs took on good stability. FL intensity of N-CDs generally did not change after 6 consecutive measurements in Fig. S4e, demonstating that N-CDs possessed great FL photostability. Moreover, the storage stability was assessed by testing the FL intensity of N-CDs within 36 days. The emission wavelength of N-CDs is neither significantly shifted nor the fluorescence intensity is significantly reduced in Fig. S4f, showing good storage stability. The aforementioned results reveal that the synthetic biomass N-CDs possessed outstanding fluorescence stability under different conditions.

Fig. 2a shows the fluorescence spectra before and after adding Fe2+ to N-CDs aqueous solution. The illustration in Fig. 2a shows the photos in the absence or presence of Fe2+ under 365 nm UV light. The presence of 10 mM Fe2+ lead to a significant decrease in the FL intensity of N-CDs at 440 nm in Fig. 2a, which shows that Fe2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. In order to explore the detection ability of N-CDs towards Fe2+, the response time of N-CDs to Fe2+ was studied. When 10 mM Fe2+ is added, the FL signal decays rapidly within 0.5 min in Fig. 2b. After 1 min, the FL intensity does not change significantly and gradually reaches a constant value, indicating a fast fluorescence response towards Fe2+.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the response range and sensitivity of the N-CDs were assessed after addition of different concentrations of Fe2+ solution (0–10−2 M) to N-CDs aqueous solution. Fig. 2c records the FL response of the above solution. The FL intensity of N-CDs gradually decreases with the increase of Fe2+ concentration, illustrating that Fe2+ display an obvious quenching effect on N-CDs. The quenching efficiency can be fitted by Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F=1 +KSV[Q]) [39]. According to the formula relation, the curve of F0/F as a function of [Q] should be linear. But the fitting curve of F0/F and [Q] shows linear deviation in Fig. 2d, manifesting the possibility of static quenching (SQ) and dynamic quenching (DQ) [40,41]. However, in SQ, the KSV is explained as ground-state association constant for N-CDs binding to Fe2+. For DQ, KSV is in the Eq. (3):
(3)
K
sv
=
k
q
×
τ
0
Where, kq is the molecular quenching rate constant and τ0 is the FL lifetime before addition of Fe2+. Among them, KSV= 0.0058 µM−1 and τ0 = 13.56 ns. kq was calculated to be 4.28 × 1013 M−1 s−1. This value was illogically high than 1 × 1010 M−1 s−1. Thus, static quenching may be due to the discovered quenching process [42,43]. To obtain the detection limit (LOD) and detection range, calibration diagrams were gained by using the modified Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F=1 +KSVlog[Q]). The fluorescence quenching efficiency F0/F proved a fine linear relationship with the logarithm of Fe2+ concentration in the range of 2–625 μM (R2 =0.9858), and the linear regression equation is F0/F= 1.02 + 0.1 log[c] (μM) (illustration in Fig. 2d). The detection limit (LOD) is calculated as 0.063 μM according to the Eq. (4):
(4)
LOD =
3 σ
S

Table S1 shows analytical performance of the present method with the other reported methods for the detection of Fe2+. In comparison with the results reported in the previous literature, it can be found that N-CDs in this study has wide linear range and low LOD value to Fe2+.

To study the selectivity and specificity of N-CDs towards Fe2+, the FL characteristics of N-CDs solution over other competing cations were tested, the fluorescence screening experiment was repeated for three times. It can be seen from Fig. 3a, with the addition of Fe2+, the FL of N-CDs is gradually quenched, while the normalized FL intensity of N-CDs containing other cations has little change. Fig. S5 shows a photo of N-CDs aqueous solution containing 10 mM different metal ions under an ultraviolet lamp. It can be seen that under UV irradiation, the FL of N-CDs is obviously quenched attribute to the presence of Fe2+, indicating that N-CDs has good selectivity for Fe2+. It shows that Fe2+ can be easily detected by N-CDs due to N-CDs has strong affinity with Fe2+. In addition, the anti-interference performance of N-CDs probe coexisting with the above metal ions was also tested. Compared with the blank sample, the FL intensity of the N-CDs was not significantly inhibited or enhanced after the interfering metal ions containing Fe2+ added in Fig. 3b. The results show that the existence of the above metal ions does not affect the fluorescence detection of Fe2+ by N-CDs, which proves that the detection method has excellent anti-interference performance for the detection of Fe2+.

In order to demonstrate the quenching mechanism of N-CDs in Fe2+ sensing, spectral analysis and fluorescence lifetime measurement were carried out. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that there is sufficient overlap among the excitation spectrum, emission spectrum, the absorption spectrum of Fe2+ and N-CDs with the addition of Fe2+, indicating that the quenching may be due to the internal filtering effect (IFE) [44,45].

The FL quenching mechanism of N-CDs in Fe2+ assay was revealed. We measured the FL lifetime with and without of Fe2+. It can be seen from Fig. 3d, the fluorescence attenuation curve detected at 440 nm can be fitted with a double exponential formula. After calculation, the FL lifetime of N-CDs aqueous solution before and after adding Fe2+ is 13.56 ns and 14.31 ns, respectively. For the static mechanism, τ0/τ = 1 [46,47]. As a result, this result suggests a quenching associated with a static mechanism, the conclusion is consistent with the above analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ ion in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the feasibility of N-CDs for detecting of Fe2+, it was used to test Fe2+ in tap water samples. The recovery rate was determined by standard addition approache, and the results were detailed in Table S2. The recoveries of Fe2+ in the samples ranged from 98.9 % to102.3 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were less than 0.6 % in Table S2. The experiment results proved that the method is suitable for the determination of Fe2+ in tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay and application of cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the application of N-CDs as a fluorescent probe for cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of Hela cells was tested by standard MTT method. After co-incubation with different concentrations of N-CDs, it can be found in Fig. S6, the survival rate of Hela cells is more than 95 % (1/50), indicating that N-CDs has relatively low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which means that it has a bright application prospect in biomedical field.

To further prove its feasibility in biological application, cell imaging experiment based on N-CDs was carried out and cell images were recorded with laser confocal microscope. The image obtained after Hela cells incubated with N-CDs at 37 °C for 5 h in Fig. 4. Through comparison, it is found that the cell morphology has no obvious change. The cells showed excellent cell viability and evenly distributed, indicating that the cytotoxicity of N-CDs is negligible. When the excitation wavelength was 405 nm and 488 nm, Hela cells incubated with N-CDs emitted strong bright blue and green fluorescence, respectively. In the control group, Hela cells without N-CDs were imaged, and no fluorescence signal was discovered. The results showed that N-CDs successfully penetrated the cell membrane and transferred into the cell through endocytosis, as well as could clearly label Hela cells. In addition, there was no significant variation in cell morphology, demonstating that the cytotoxicity of N-CDs was negligible. Above results show that N-CDs as a fluorescent indicator has a good application prospect in biomedical field.

In conclusion, N-CDs with high QY (58.66 %) were successfully prepared by one-step simple and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method. N-CDs show strong blue fluorescence, wide pH value, easy to dissolve in water, outstanding stability, excellent ionic strength and photobleaching performance. Because the FL intensity of N-CDs can be quickly, sensitively and selectively quenched by Fe2+, N-CDs can be used as a new fluorescent probe to selective detect Fe2+. And a wide linear relationship is observed in the range of 2–625 μM with the LOD of 0.063 μM. Static quenching and internal filtration effect collectively affected the ﬂuorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe2+. Besides, N-CDs possess low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be used as an eco-friendly fluorescent probe for multicolor imaging sensing in vivo or in vitro. In short, due to low cost, simplicity and sensitivity, N-CDs may be widely used in biomedical research in the future.

Alan Meng: Methodology, Conceptualization. Yue Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhenjiang Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision. Liying Sheng: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, 52102106, Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, ZR2020QE063, the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004, and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.130580.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

Chem. Soc. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quenching of coumarin emission by CdSe and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots: Implications for fluorescence reporting
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Baride, D. Engebretson, M.T. Berry, P.S. May, Quenching of coumarin emission by CdSe and CdSe/ZnS quantum dots: Implications for fluorescence reporting, J. Lumin. 141 (2013) 99–105. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2013.03.027.
